title: Continuous Bladder Irrigation (CBI)
subtitle: The nurse manages CBI through a three-way catheter — using sterile saline, titrating the rate to keep urine pink and clear, subtracting irrigant to find true urine output, and recognizing an obstructing clot.

patient.name: Bladder Irrigation
patient.admitDate: The nurse manages continuous bladder irrigation through a three-way catheter, often after prostate surgery.

labs.r: Faster if red, slower if clear.

nursesNote: The nurse manages continuous bladder irrigation (CBI), which runs sterile normal saline through a three-way (triple-lumen) catheter to flush clots and keep the catheter patent, most often after a transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP). The irrigation rate is titrated to the urine color: it is increased when drainage is bright red or has clots and slowed as the urine clears toward light pink. Drainage output always exceeds the irrigant infused, so the nurse subtracts the volume of irrigant from the total output to record true urine output. Pink-tinged urine that gradually clears, with a few small clots early, is expected. A sudden drop or absence of output with a distended, painful bladder and spasms suggests an obstructing clot; the nurse checks the tubing for kinks and clots, increases the rate or performs manual irrigation per order, and notifies the provider if output does not return. The CBI is not stopped when the urine turns bloody; instead the rate is increased. The bag is kept below the bladder, a closed sterile system is maintained, and bladder spasms are treated with prescribed antispasmodics.
providerOrders: Run sterile normal saline CBI through the three-way catheter
Titrate the rate to keep the urine light pink and clear
Record true urine output by subtracting the irrigant volume
Keep output greater than input; report no output with bladder distension
Increase the rate for bright-red drainage; do not stop the CBI

opts.text: CBI uses a three-way catheter to flush the bladder
Sterile normal saline is the irrigating solution
Drainage output is always greater than the irrigant infused
Bright red drainage with clots should be reported
To find true urine output, add the irrigant to the drainage
The irrigation is stopped if the urine turns bloody
The rate is increased when drainage is bright red
Output less than input may mean the catheter is obstructed
No output with distension and spasm suggests a clot
Subtracting the irrigant from total output gives true urine
Bladder spasms are common after prostate surgery
Pink-tinged urine that is clearing is an emergency

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Output suddenly stops; the bladder is distended and the patient has spasms

opts.text: Assess the patient, bladder, and drainage system
Check the tubing for kinks and clots
Increase the rate or perform manual irrigation per order
Notify the provider if output does not return
Document the findings

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Expected finding or notify the provider?

opts.text: Pink-tinged urine that is gradually clearing
Bright red drainage with numerous clots
Output that exceeds the irrigant infused
A few small clots early after surgery
No output with a distended, painful bladder
Drainage that lightens as the rate is titrated
Severe, unrelieved bladder spasms
Sudden drop in output with increasing clots

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The drainage becomes bright red with clots
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the patient and vital signs
Increase the CBI rate as ordered
Check for and clear clots or kinks in the tubing
Notify the provider if bleeding continues
Document output and findings
Using sterile normal saline for the irrigation
Stopping the CBI because the urine turned bloody
Subtracting the irrigant from output for true urine
Counting the irrigant as urine output
Increasing the rate when drainage is bright red
Ignoring a distended bladder with no output
Keeping the drainage bag below the bladder
Letting the tubing kink under the patient
